architecture.summary: A comprehensive blueprint organizing Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) into a modular framework combining three pipelines (inference, training, data generation). Inference constructs reasoning structures (chains, trees, graphs, nested forms) by iteratively applying operators (generate, select, aggregate, prune, restructure, refine) guided by reasoning strategies (MCTS, Beam Search, ensemble methods). Policy and value models score candidate steps; training incorporates both supervised (PRM800K) and self-learning phases with outcome-based or process-based supervision. Data generation mirrors inference to enable self-play-like continuous improvement. The framework unifies components through operators (structure, traversal, update) applied to reasoning structures under different reasoning strategies.

claims.statement: RLMs combine LLMs with advanced reasoning mechanisms through integration of reinforcement learning, search heuristics, and structured exploration

The blueprint encompasses diverse reasoning structures (chains, trees, graphs, and nested forms), reasoning strategies (Monte Carlo Tree Search, Beam Search, ensemble methods), and supervision schemes (Outcome-Based and Process-Based Supervision)
Existing schemes such as LLaMA-Berry, QwQ, Journey Learning, and Graph of Thoughts fit as special cases within the unified blueprint
Multi-phase training for policy and value models is key to effective RLM construction
Familiar training distributions (distributions close to the model's pretraining distribution) are important for RLM performance

RLMs enable extrapolation beyond training data boundaries by combining structured exploration with LLM knowledge, in contrast to standard LLMs which primarily perform interpolation within their training distribution

Reasoning steps can vary in granularity from single tokens to entire reasoning chains or logical arguments; coarse-grained steps simplify data preparation and enhance interpretability while reducing computational overhead, whereas fine-grained steps enable incorporation of model uncertainty and advanced decoding schemes

A modular implementation (x1) has been created to simplify development and experimentation with new RLM architectures, supporting training, inference, synthetic data generation, batch processing, and cloud deployment integration
